title: Light a Candle for Them Daily
subtitle: A small daily grief ritual for keeping the connection alive without being overwhelmed by it
excerpt: A brief daily ritual for grief. One candle, lit at a regular time, to mark the person who is gone. The practice holds the grief in a small, bounded container. One minute a day, so it doesn't have to leak into everything else.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Daily candle-lighting as a grief practice appears across many cultures and religious traditions as a way of marking the ongoing presence of the dead. The five-part ritual structure draws on the framework in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use as a daily practice for active grief, or on anniversaries, or when you want to mark someone's presence in your life without requiring a larger act of mourning.

content.text: A small daily ritual for keeping the person who has died present without being consumed by the grief. One candle, lit once a day, at a regular time. The practice gives grief a bounded container so that it doesn't have to leak into everything else.

Keep a candle in a fixed place for this practice only. Not the kitchen candle, not the decorative one. This candle is theirs. Light it at the same time each day, morning, evening, whenever fits, and blow it out after a minute or two. The repetition is the ritual.
I am ready to release the idea that I should be over this by now. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow this small daily practice to hold what I cannot say. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Watch the flame for one minute. You don't have to think of them deliberately. If they come, let them come. If the grief comes, let it be there for this one minute. It has somewhere to be.
They were here. They are in my heart. This flame is for them.
Say it or think it, then blow the candle out. Go on with the day.

Written for homemade.education. Daily candle-lighting for the dead appears across many cultures and spiritual traditions as a way of marking an ongoing connection with someone who has died. The five-part ritual structure draws on the ceremony shape in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).
